Client 2: Net, like what hits the checking?
Advisor: Good catch—gross, so before taxes and deductions. We can do net later.
Client 1: Gross combined is about $103,650 a year.
Advisor: Got it—about $103,650 gross household. And Daniel, that’s mostly you plus Emily’s salary, right?
Client 2: Yes—well, plus his other income streams.

Advisor: Great. I’m going to pivot to spending for a second. About how much do you spend per month, all-in—mortgage or rent, utilities, groceries, travel, everything?
Client 2: We’re around $3,500 a month.
Client 1: No, Em—sorry—closer to $3,200. The $3,500 was when we were traveling more.
Client 2: Okay, fair. $3,200 is the normal month.
Advisor: Thanks for sorting that out. So ongoing monthly expenses are about $3,200. And do you have any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loans, credit cards you’re carrying?

Client 2: Net, like what hits the checking?
Advisor: Good catch—gross, so before taxes and deductions. We can do net later.
Client 1: Gross combined is about $103,650 a year.
Advisor: Got it—about $103,650 gross household. And Daniel, that’s mostly you plus Emily’s salary, right?
Client 2: Yes—well, plus his other income streams.

Advisor: Great. Now let’s talk investable assets—everything that’s not the house. Roughly what do you have across brokerage, retirement accounts, bank cash, and any private investments?
Client 2: A lot? I don’t know—six hundred something?
Client 1: Total investable is about $693,550.
Advisor: Okay—$693,550 investable assets total. Let’s break that into the buckets so I understand what’s where.
Advisor: First, do you have a joint taxable brokerage account—non-retirement—somewhere like a retirement platform or custodian?

Advisor: All good—so monthly debt cost is $0. Let me note that down.
Advisor: Since you brought up the house—what’s the home worth roughly, Daniel and Emily?
Client 1: About $599,300.
Advisor: Great. Now let’s talk investable assets—everything that’s not the house. Roughly what do you have across brokerage, retirement accounts, bank cash, and any private investments?
Client 2: A lot? I don’t know—six hundred something?

Client 2: Okay, yeah—moderate, but with guardrails.
Client 1: That’s the right way to say it.
Advisor: Perfect. And just so I’m not assuming—are we working together here in the U.S. RIA world, meaning a registered investment advisor relationship, not like an insurance-only setup?
Client 2: Yes, U.S. RIA—fee advisor, not insurance-only.
Client 1: Right, that’s what we’re looking for.

Client 2: Net, like what hits the checking?
Advisor: Good catch—gross, so before taxes and deductions. We can do net later.
Client 1: Gross combined is about $103,650 a year.
Advisor: Got it—about $103,650 gross household. And Daniel, that’s mostly you plus Emily’s salary, right?
Client 2: Yes—well, plus his other income streams.

Advisor: Hi Daniel, hi Emily—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to come in now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. Honestly, we just want a clean plan—markets have been jumpy and I’m tired of guessing.
Client 2: Hi. And I want it in plain English, because I get lost when it’s all account types and tax stuff.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Going back to what you just said, Daniel—when you say “clean plan,” is it retirement timing, cash flow, or just making sure you’re protected?

Client 1: Yeah, zero dependents at this point.
Advisor: Okay—no dependents. Let me jot that down. One sec.
Advisor: Now, risk tolerance—when markets get ugly, how do you two generally react? Would you say conservative, moderate, or aggressive?
Client 1: Moderate. I’m not trying to swing for the fences, but I don’t want everything in cash either.
Client 2: I’d say conservative—like, I don’t want to lose money.

Client 2: I mean… we’re probably like, what, thirty-two percent?
Client 1: No—hang on—it's higher. We’re in the thirty-seven percent bracket.
Advisor: Okay, 37%. Emily, you were thinking 32, but Daniel you’re saying 37%—37 is the one you want me to use?
Client 2: Yeah—sorry, I always mix up the bracket versus what we actually pay overall.
Advisor: Totally fair—marginal bracket versus effective rate. So we’ll use 37% as the bracket.

Advisor: Hi Daniel, hi Emily—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to come in now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. Honestly, we just want a clean plan—markets have been jumpy and I’m tired of guessing.
Client 2: Hi. And I want it in plain English, because I get lost when it’s all account types and tax stuff.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Going back to what you just said, Daniel—when you say “clean plan,” is it retirement timing, cash flow, or just making sure you’re protected?
Client 1: All of it, but cash flow and taxes first. Then protection.

Client 2: Yeah, no debt. Just the usual monthly bills.
Advisor: Perfect. Let me jot that down—no liabilities.
Advisor: Now, zooming out to the household basics for planning: Daniel and Emily, are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Client 1: Married, yep.

Advisor: Hi Daniel, hi Emily—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to come in now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. Honestly, we just want a clean plan—markets have been jumpy and I’m tired of guessing.
Client 2: Hi. And I want it in plain English, because I get lost when it’s all account types and tax stuff.

Client 2: Yes—well, plus his other income streams.
Advisor: Right, we’ll break those out. Daniel, what do you have coming in personally from work—your gross annual?
Client 1: My W-2-ish income is $68,700 a year.
Advisor: And Emily, yours from the school district—ballpark gross?
Client 2: About $35,000—$34,950 if I’m being exact.

Client 1: Busy, but good. Okay—yeah.
Advisor: Busy but good—yeah, I hear you. Going back to what you said earlier about having no liabilities, I just want to separate “no debt” from “no mortgage,” because people sometimes mean different things.
Advisor: Daniel, on the home itself—do you own a primary residence, and what’s it worth roughly today?
Client 1: Yes, we own our primary place—roughly $599,300, give or take.
Client 2: I’d just say about six hundred thousand, yeah.

Advisor: Hi Daniel, hi Emily—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to come in now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. Honestly, we just want a clean plan—markets have been jumpy and I’m tired of guessing.
Client 2: Hi. And I want it in plain English, because I get lost when it’s all account types and tax stuff.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Going back to what you just said, Daniel—when you say “clean plan,” is it retirement timing, cash flow, or just making sure you’re protected?

Client 2: I mean—primary earner, yes. But we decide together.
Advisor: Totally—primary earner versus decision-making, I get it. I’ll note Daniel as primary, and you two as joint decision-makers.
Advisor: Daniel, what do you do day-to-day—are we calling you a business owner?
Client 1: Yes, business owner.
Advisor: Okay.

Client 2: And I’m Client 2, yes.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, Daniel, I also want to confirm your date of birth—what’s your birthday?
Client 1: October 24th, 1964.
Client 2: Wait—1964? I thought you were '63.
Client 1: No—'64. You always do that.

Client 1: Busy, but good. Okay—yeah.
Advisor: Busy but good—yeah, I hear you. Okay, going back to that gross-versus-net thing from a second ago, I want to make sure I’ve got Emily’s work details cleanly.
Advisor: Emily, you’re still working, right—currently employed?
Client 2: Yes, I’m employed—still working full-time.
Advisor: Got it. And just to put a plain label on it, what field are you in—education, healthcare, something else?

Client 1: My W-2-ish income is $68,700 a year.
Advisor: And Emily, yours from the school district—ballpark gross?

Advisor: Perfect. Now, you mentioned other income streams, Emily. Daniel, do you have monthly income like Social Security or investment income coming in?

Advisor: Emily, you’re still working, right—currently employed?
Client 2: Yes, I’m employed—still working full-time.
Advisor: Got it. And just to put a plain label on it, what field are you in—education, healthcare, something else?
Client 2: Education. I work at a school.

Client 2: Yes, employed. Still working.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. Now, I’m going to ask something that sounds picky: Emily, what’s your date of birth?

Client 2: Yes, I’m the spouse—spouse/partner, yeah.
Client 1: Right.

Client 1: Yeah, that sounds right.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, you mentioned other income streams, Emily. Daniel, do you have monthly income like Social Security or investment income coming in?
Client 1: Yes. Social Security is coming in monthly—annualized it’s about $69,450.
Client 2: Wait, annualized means… you multiplied the monthly by twelve?
